summary: Fowler argues that spreadsheets represent the most common programming language because they're used by non-programmers for surprisingly complex tasks. He introduces 'Illustrative Programming' as a concept where program execution is fused with its definition, making the output visible while editing. While this approach helps lay programmers understand behavior through concrete examples, it tends to de-emphasize program structure, leading to unmaintainable systems. The challenge for future environments is to maintain good program structure while preserving the illustrative benefits. Fowler sees potential in Language Workbenches that could build illustrative DSLs to engage lay programmers without sacrificing code quality.
key_points: Spreadsheets succeed as programming tools because they fuse program definition with execution, showing calculated results rather than formulae
Illustrative programming differs from REPLs by putting examples front-and-center rather than as an exploration tool
The main weakness is that illustrative environments de-emphasize program structure, leading to copy-paste programming and unmaintainable systems
The inability to create new abstractions in UI builders causes programs to become tangled as they can only illustrate built-in concepts
Language Workbenches offer potential for building illustrative DSLs that could engage lay programmers while maintaining structure
money_quotes: Whenever someone talks about some new environment that's going to allow people to specify complex behavior 'without programming' I mention COBOL, which was originally designed to get rid of programmers.
We've used illustrative programming for years, but we've not paid enough attention to it.
The program retreats to the background, peeping out only when we want to explore a part of the illustration.
They do a good job of revealing what a program does, but de-emphasizes program structure.

The challenge for future illustrative programming environments is to help develop a well structured program behind the illustrations - although the illustrations may also make us rethink what a well structured program is.
APIs in programming languages scale much better, with several orders of magnitude more density before they become hard to navigate.
key_insight: The power of spreadsheets lies in fusing program definition with execution output, but this illustrative approach's emphasis on visible results over program structure creates a fundamental tension that future programming environments must resolve.
